captions.text: Cruising — radar tracks the car ahead continuously.
Lead car brakes hard. Warning at 2.0s time-to-collision.
No driver response → automatic braking at 0.8s TTC.
Car stops with a safety gap. Loop restarts.

usefulIf: You drive on highways or fast dual carriageways
Heavy traffic with sudden stops
New or occasional drivers in the family
notUsefulIf: Mostly low-speed city driving under 40 km/h
You already drive very defensively with large following gaps
context.frequencyTemplate: Rarely in pure city — matters on {highwayTrips}/week highway runs

appendUseful: Your profile includes regular highway trips — AEB pays off there
Extra safety margin while you're still building reflexes

steps.label: Returns to set speed when clear

captions.text: Set speed 100 km/h — radar locks a following gap.
Lead car slows; your car eases off automatically.
Lead car clears the lane → smooth return to set speed.

usefulIf: Weekly highway commutes (ORR, expressways)
Long family road trips
Tired evening drives home
notUsefulIf: Under 15% highway driving
You prefer full manual control

appendUseful: At your highway share, you'll use this almost every long drive
Hyderabad ORR runs are ideal ACC territory

captions.text: Camera reads lane markings ahead.
Car drifts toward the line without indicating…
Gentle counter-steer nudges it back to centre.

usefulIf: High-speed highway cruising
Long drives where fatigue sets in
Wide lanes on expressways
notUsefulIf: Narrow city lanes with constant overtaking
Faded or missing road markings in your area

appendUseful: High annual km means more hours where lane-keep reduces fatigue

captions.text: Radar watches the zone your mirrors can't.
A two-wheeler enters the blind spot — mirror icon lights.
Indicate now and the warning escalates. Wait, then move.

usefulIf: Frequent lane changes on highways
City driving with heavy two-wheeler traffic
Wide C-pillars that block rear view
notUsefulIf: Rarely change lanes
You always shoulder-check religiously

appendUseful: Bengaluru's lane-splitting traffic makes BSM especially valuable
Western Express Highway merges are classic blind-spot moments

steps.label: Car holds speed, gap and lane

captions.text: Level 2 = adaptive cruise + lane centering, together.
The car holds speed, gap and lane position itself.
Hands stay on the wheel — you supervise, it assists.

usefulIf: Regular 100+ km highway trips
You value reduced fatigue on long drives
Tech-forward buyers who will actually use assist
notUsefulIf: Almost entirely city driving
You don't trust or want semi-autonomous features

appendUseful: Your driving mix justifies L2 on monthly highway runs

captions.text: On the highway, tap SET at 100 km/h.
Lift your foot — throttle holds steady.
Tap brake or RES to adjust. Simple, no radar needed.

usefulIf: Open highway stretches without stop-go traffic
Long drives where leg fatigue matters
Fuel efficiency — steadier throttle saves a bit
notUsefulIf: Dense city traffic with constant braking
You already have adaptive cruise on your shortlist

appendUseful: Worth having — you'll reach for it on ORR and expressway legs

captions.text: Stopped on a slope — foot on brake.
Release brake — hill-hold keeps you still for ~2s.
Clutch/accelerate smoothly. No rollback into the car behind.

usefulIf: Parking ramps and basement exits
Flyovers and inclined signals
Manual transmission (if equipped) or stop-start on slopes
notUsefulIf: Flat city with rare inclines
You're very confident with clutch control

appendUseful: Hyderabad flyovers and ORR ramps — you'll feel this weekly
Koramangala and Indiranagar ramps reward hill-hold
Removes one of the scariest manual-driving moments

captions.text: Shade closed — cabin feels like a normal car.
Slide the shade — panoramic glass brightens the whole cabin.
Evening highway: open vent, not full sun. Ambience upgrade.

usefulIf: You care about cabin ambience and road trips
Rear passengers (kids enjoy the view)
Mild climates where you won't bake in summer
notUsefulIf: Parked outdoors all day in 40°C+ cities
You never open it after month one

appendNotUseful: Delhi summers — you'll keep the shade shut May–July

captions.text: Perforated seat leather lets air pass through the cushion.
Built-in fans push air — your back stops sticking in summer.
The comfort feature owners actually use daily in hot cities.

usefulIf: Hot, humid cities — Hyderabad, Chennai, Mumbai summers
Long commutes in stop-start traffic
You wear formal shirts and hate back sweat
notUsefulIf: Mild hill-station climate year-round
You rarely drive in peak afternoon heat

appendUseful: Summer afternoons make this one of the highest-ROI comfort upgrades
Humidity + heat — ventilated seats beat any sunroof

captions.text: Set 22°C once — the system manages fan speed and compressor.
Cabin cools down without constant knob fiddling.
Temperature stays stable through traffic and sun load.

usefulIf: Daily drivers who hate adjusting AC every few minutes
Shared cars with different temperature preferences
Long highway runs where consistency matters
notUsefulIf: You only take short trips and manual AC is fine
Base trim budget is the priority

captions.text: Second row gets its own vents from the centre tunnel.
Direct airflow reaches rear passengers — not just front-row cold.
Essential for families in Indian heat — rear seats stay usable.

usefulIf: Regularly carry family in the back seat
Kids or parents sit rear on long trips
You run the AC hard in summer
notUsefulIf: Mostly solo driving
Two-row SUV used as a 2-seater

appendUseful: Delhi summers — rear passengers without vents suffer fast

captions.text: Single electric glass panel above the front row.
Tilt or slide open — cabin feels brighter and airier.
Mostly a desirability upgrade; vent position beats full open in summer.

usefulIf: You want a brighter cabin on evening drives
Resale and brochure appeal matter to you
Occasional open-air feel without a convertible
notUsefulIf: Parked in direct sun all day — adds heat
You never open it after the first month

appendNotUseful: Peak summer — shade stays shut most days

captions.text: Synthetic leather look with stitched panels and easier upkeep.
Spills sit on the surface instead of soaking into fabric.
Wipe clean in seconds — handy with kids and daily commute.

usefulIf: Kids, pets, or frequent food/drink in the car
You care about resale looking well-kept
Premium feel without real leather maintenance
notUsefulIf: You prefer breathable fabric in hot weather
Leatherette can feel warm without ventilation

captions.text: Electric motors adjust height, reach, and backrest angle.
Fine-tune driving position without manual levers under the seat.
Shared cars and long commutes — everyone finds their fit faster.

usefulIf: Multiple drivers share the same car
Long commute — small posture tweaks reduce fatigue
You want easy reach to pedals and wheel
notUsefulIf: Single driver who set manual seat once
Short city hops only

captions.text: Disc brakes on all four wheels — not drums at the rear.
Even braking force across corners under repeated stops.
Better fade resistance on ghat descents and spirited driving.

usefulIf: Regular ghat and hill descents
Spirited driving or heavy loaded stops
You upgrade from a base trim with rear drums
notUsefulIf: Pure city driving at moderate speeds
You never push brakes hard enough to fade

appendUseful: Highway off-ramps and long descents reward all-disc setups

captions.text: Paddles behind the wheel let you shift manually in an automatic.
Downshift on a hill descent — engine braking saves the foot brake.
Hold a gear for overtakes; otherwise most owners rarely touch them.

usefulIf: Ghat routes where engine braking helps
Enthusiasts who want manual override
DCT/AT drivers who hold gears for overtakes
notUsefulIf: Flat city commute only
You never touch them after the first week

captions.text: Eco softens throttle for traffic and fuel savings.
Normal is the daily default — balanced response.
Sport sharpens throttle and sometimes steering — modest but noticeable.

usefulIf: You like tailoring throttle feel
Sport mode for highway merges
Eco in stop-start traffic
notUsefulIf: You leave it in Normal forever anyway
Differences feel too subtle to matter to you

captions.text: 17-inch alloy wheels — lighter and sharper looking than steel.
Diamond-cut face catches light — the brochure look buyers notice.
Slightly firmer ride than smaller wheels; marginally better grip.

usefulIf: Style and road presence matter to you
You want the top-trim look on mid variants
Slightly wider tyre contact patch
notUsefulIf: Bad-road comfort is priority — smaller wheels ride softer
Alloy repair cost worries you on potholed roads

captions.text: Each tyre reports pressure to the car.
Pressure drops slowly — yellow warning on the cluster.
Stop and inflate before highway speeds or a sidewall failure.

usefulIf: Highway driving at sustained speeds
Pothole-heavy roads that knock alignment
You don't check tyre pressure monthly
notUsefulIf: You already check pressures every fortnight
Pure city driving under 60 km/h

appendUseful: Highway heat + under-inflation is a real blowout risk — TPMS catches it early

captions.text: Wet road — you swerve to avoid an obstacle.
Tail steps out — ESC detects yaw.
Selective braking pulls the car back in line. You may not even notice.

usefulIf: Monsoon driving on highways
Emergency lane changes
Every driver — it's passive safety

appendUseful: Mumbai monsoons + expressway aquaplaning — ESC is non-negotiable

captions.text: System monitors crash sensors constantly.
Frontal collision registered at the bumper.
Curtain, front, and side airbags inflate in 8 milliseconds.
Controlled deflation cushions occupants and absorbs energy.

usefulIf: Family with rear passengers
Highway speeds where crash energy is higher
You want maximum passive protection
notUsefulIf: Rarely carry passengers — still valuable in solo crashes

appendUseful: More airbag coverage while reflexes are still developing

captions.text: 10.25-inch high-definition screen sits in your direct line of sight.
Tap the right shortcut button to split the screen.
Split Screen Mode: view navigation maps and media playback side by side.
Tap the left sidebar to access camera shortcuts.
Reverse camera feed displays active parking guides and sensor indicators.

usefulIf: You use Google Maps / Android Auto / Apple CarPlay daily
Enjoy clear and crisp reverse/360 camera feeds
Prefer clean, button-free dashboard controls
notUsefulIf: You prefer tactile knobs and physical buttons for AC/volume
Don't use phone mirroring navigation

captions.text: Place phone in the central dashboard charging tray.
Phone connects automatically via Bluetooth and 5GHz Wi-Fi.
Maps, music, and voice controls project instantly on the 10"+ display.

usefulIf: Daily navigation via Google/Apple Maps
Short trips where plugging in feels tedious
Clean dashboard aesthetic
notUsefulIf: You rarely use phone mirroring
Your phone battery dies without wired charging

appendUseful: High km means hundreds of cable-free hops in and out

captions.text: Fully digital cluster replaces analog dials with a crisp LCD panel.
Speed, fuel, and trip data render with sharper graphics and smoother needles.
Navigation turn prompts and media info can sit in your direct sightline.

usefulIf: Tech-first buyers who like modern cabin feel
You use navigation constantly and want turn info near the wheel
You prefer configurable layouts over fixed analog dials
notUsefulIf: Analog gauges are fine for your driving style
You rarely look at the cluster beyond speed

captions.text: SIM-linked app connects to your car from anywhere.
Tap remote pre-cool before you walk to the parking lot.
Cabin starts cooling in summer — the feature owners actually use daily.

usefulIf: Hot-city parking where cabin heat is brutal
Parents tracking a shared family car
You like locating the car in big mall basements
notUsefulIf: You never use phone apps beyond maps
Your parking is always shaded or underground with easy access

appendUseful: Summer pre-cool is genuinely useful before you reach a sun-baked car

steps.label: Battery tops up on the go

captions.text: Drop your phone on the Qi pad in the centre console.
Coil aligns and tops up battery without a cable.
Handy with wireless CarPlay — keeps charge from draining on long drives.

usefulIf: You use wireless CarPlay or Android Auto daily
Short hops where plugging in feels tedious
Phone battery drains fast with navigation running
notUsefulIf: You prefer wired charging for faster top-ups
Your phone lacks Qi charging

captions.text: More speakers and amplification across doors, dash, and subwoofer.
Music staging opens up — clearer vocals and tighter bass at moderate volume.
Best felt on highway cruises; road noise still limits gains at very high speed.

usefulIf: Music-first commuters who drive alone
You listen to podcasts or music on every trip
You notice cabin sound quality in test drives
notUsefulIf: Audio quality is low priority vs safety or space
Most driving is noisy city traffic where differences blur

steps.label: Parking guide lines frame the gap

captions.text: Shift to reverse and the rear view fills the screen.
Dynamic guide lines show where the car will track.
You spot low walls, scooters, and curbs much earlier.

usefulIf: You park in tight apartment basements
Your family car often reverses around scooters and pillars
You share the car with newer drivers
notUsefulIf: You almost always have open parking space
You already rely on a crisp 360 camera system

appendUseful: Basement parking and narrow apartment slots make this feel useful every week

steps.label: Multiple cameras stitch a top view

captions.text: Cameras around the car build a stitched bird's-eye view.
You can judge all four corners instead of one mirror at a time.
Useful when walls, pillars, and scooters crowd the parking slot.

usefulIf: You park in premium malls, offices, or tight apartment slots
You buy a wider SUV and want corner confidence
You care about alloy and bumper protection
notUsefulIf: Your car is small and parking is usually easy
You rarely deal with narrow urban parking

appendUseful: Builds confidence quickly when learning the car's exact corners

steps.label: Beep rate rises as the gap shrinks
Stop before the bumper kisses it

captions.text: Creep toward a pillar or wall in a tight slot.
The beeps get faster as the nose closes in.
You stop with less bumper guessing and fewer parking scrapes.

usefulIf: You park nose-first into tight spaces
Your car has a tall bonnet or poor front-edge visibility
Shared family drivers use the same car
notUsefulIf: You mostly reverse into open parking bays
You already have a trustworthy 360 camera

appendUseful: Tight urban parking makes front sensors more useful than buyers expect

steps.label: You stay visible without thinking about it

captions.text: Daylight fades as you enter rain, dusk, or a covered ramp.
The sensor triggers the headlamps automatically.
Good convenience because you stay visible even when you forget the stalk.

usefulIf: You drive at dawn, dusk, or during monsoons
The car is shared and people forget to switch lights on
You frequently enter tunnels, ramps, and covered parking
notUsefulIf: You drive almost entirely in daylight and are disciplined about lights

appendUseful: More time on the road means more low-light moments where this quietly helps

steps.label: You keep both hands focused on driving

captions.text: First drops land on the glass during a sudden shower.
The sensor starts the wipers and adjusts the speed.
Small luxury, but nice in monsoon traffic and messy weather.

usefulIf: You drive in monsoon-heavy cities
You want fewer small controls to manage in bad weather
You like premium-feeling convenience touches
notUsefulIf: You are picky and prefer to control wiper speed yourself
Your city rarely sees meaningful rain

appendUseful: This feels much more valuable once Mumbai rain starts hammering the glass

steps.label: Approach the car with key in pocket

captions.text: Walk up with the key in your pocket or bag.
Touch the handle and the car unlocks.
Push-button start is one of those things you miss once you've used it daily.

usefulIf: You do many short hops in and out of the car
You often carry bags, laptops, or groceries
You want the car to feel a class above
notUsefulIf: You don't mind using a conventional key
You care more about hard-value features than cabin convenience

appendUseful: Lots of weekly stop-start errands make this feel nicer than it sounds on paper

captions.text: A bright SUV or high-beam car fills the mirror behind you.
The mirror dims automatically instead of blasting your eyes.
Helpful on night highways, especially when glare fatigue builds up.

usefulIf: You do regular night highway runs
High-beam glare bothers you
You want small premium touches that reduce fatigue
notUsefulIf: You almost never drive at night
Rear glare rarely bothers you

appendUseful: At your highway share, this becomes more than a brochure gimmick

steps.label: Car looks more modern and visible
Tail lamps echo the same design language

captions.text: The DRLs light up with a sharper signature than basic halogen strips.
They improve daytime visibility and make the car look more premium.
Mostly an aesthetic upgrade, but it does help the car feel newer every day.

usefulIf: You care about how modern the car looks
You want stronger road presence in traffic
Night-time rear-light clarity matters to you
notUsefulIf: You only care about mechanical and ownership-value features
Exterior design is low priority for your purchase

steps.label: Rear glass fogs up or gets wet

captions.text: Rear visibility drops when mist, drizzle, or dirt builds up on the glass.
The defogger clears the rear screen line by line.
With the rear wiper working too, you regain a usable mirror view quickly.

usefulIf: You drive in rain, fog, or dusty conditions
You own an SUV or hatch where rear glass gets dirty easily
Highway rear visibility matters to you
notUsefulIf: You mostly drive in dry conditions and rarely need the rear glass clear

appendUseful: Cool mornings and drizzle make rear visibility aids feel more useful than expected
